label: Yehezkel (Ezekiel) — Part 5 — Chapters 33–39
summary_plain: YHWH appoints Yehezkel as watchman: if the watchman sees the sword coming and does not warn the people, their blood is on his hands. YHWH declares: I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked — turn back, turn back from your evil ways — why will you die, house of Yisra'EL? YHWH denounces the shepherds of Yisra'EL who feed themselves instead of the flock — I will rescue my sheep from their mouths. YHWH himself will be their shepherd. In the valley of dry bones, YHWH sets Yehezkel down among bones that are very dry. Can these bones live? YHWH commands: Prophesy to these bones — and they come together, bone to bone, sinew and flesh cover them, and the breath enters them. YHWH says: These bones are the whole house of Yisra'EL — they say our bones are dried up, our hope is lost. But I will open your graves and bring you into the land. Two sticks — Yehudah and Efrayim — become one stick in YHWH's hand. Gog of the land of Magog comes against Yisra'EL — and YHWH destroys him with fire, brimstone, and pestilence.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Ezekiel fragments (4QEzek-a, 4QEzek-b, 4QEzek-c, 11QEzek) and the Orit Ge'ez. Chapter 34 — the denunciation of false shepherds — is the foundational prophetic critique of institutional leadership. Chapter 37 — the valley of dry bones — is the defining restoration vision of the entire prophetic literature. The bones are explicitly identified: 'these bones are the whole house of Yisra'EL' (37:11). The two-sticks prophecy (37:15-28) promises the reunification of the divided kingdoms — Yehudah and Efrayim/Yosef — into one nation under one shepherd. Chapters 38-39 — the Gog and Magog oracle — describe an eschatological invasion that YHWH himself defeats. The War Scroll at Qumran (1QM) draws on this framework for its eschatological battle scenario.

key_terms.definition: Chapter 37: YHWH sets Yehezkel in a valley full of dry bones and commands him to prophesy. The bones come together, sinew and flesh cover them, breath enters them, and they live. YHWH declares: these bones are the whole house of Yisra'EL. The vision is the definitive prophetic image of national restoration.

Chapter 34: YHWH denounces the shepherds of Yisra'EL: they eat the fat, clothe themselves with wool, slaughter the fattened sheep — but they do not feed the flock. The weak they do not strengthen. The sick they do not heal. The lost they do not seek. YHWH will remove them and shepherd the flock himself.

Chapter 37:15-28: YHWH commands Yehezkel to take two sticks — one for Yehudah and one for Yosef/Efrayim — and join them into one stick. YHWH will make them one nation, no longer two kingdoms, under one king — and David my servant shall be their prince forever.

Chapters 38-39: a figure from the land of Magog who leads a coalition of nations against Yisra'EL in the latter days. YHWH destroys him with fire, brimstone, blood, and pestilence. The War Scroll at Qumran draws on this framework.

fill_blank.prompt: And he said to me: Son of man, can these ______ live? And I answered: O YHWH, you know.
fill_blank.source_quote: And he said to me: Son of man, can these bones live? And I answered: O YHWH, you know.

fill_blank.prompt: Woe to the shepherds of Yisra'EL who have been feeding ______!
fill_blank.source_quote: Son of man, prophesy against the shepherds of Yisra'EL — prophesy and say to them: Woe to the shepherds of Yisra'EL who have been feeding themselves! Should not shepherds feed the flock?

fill_blank.prompt: I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked — but that the wicked turn from his way and ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Say to them: As I live, declares YHWH, I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked, but that the wicked turn from his way and live. Turn back, turn back from your evil ways — why will you die, O house of Yisra'EL?

multiple_choice.question: Who do the dry bones represent?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Then he said to me: Son of man, these bones are the whole house of Yisra'EL. Behold, they say: Our bones are dried up, and our hope is lost — we are cut off completely.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH declare about the false shepherds?
options: He will give them another chance
He will remove them and rescue the flock from their mouths
He will promote them to kings
He will send them to the nations
multiple_choice.source_quote: I am against the shepherds, and I will require my flock at their hand and put a stop to their feeding the flock. No longer shall the shepherds feed themselves. I will rescue my flock from their mouths.

multiple_choice.question: What does the two-sticks prophecy represent?
options: The reunification of Yehudah and Efrayim into one nation
multiple_choice.source_quote: Join them one to another into one stick, that they may become one in your hand — I will make them one nation in the land, and one king shall be king over them all.

faq.question: How do the dry bones represent the scattered covenant people's restoration?
faq.answer: The dry bones vision is not a general resurrection metaphor. YHWH is explicit: 'these bones are the whole house of Yisra'EL' (37:11). The people say: 'our bones are dried up, our hope is lost, we are cut off completely.' This is the condition of a people scattered across the earth, stripped of identity, their covenant connection severed by institutional forces — the Lo-Ammi state described by Hoshea. The bones are very dry — meaning the scattering has gone on so long that the people appear beyond recovery. But YHWH commands the prophet to speak to the bones — and they come together. Bone to bone. Sinew covers them. Flesh grows. Breath enters. They stand on their feet — an exceedingly great army. The restoration is not gradual institutional reform. It is prophetic speech over dead material that YHWH reconstitutes. The two sticks that follow (37:15-28) specify the scope: both Yehudah and Yosef/Efrayim — the southern and northern kingdoms, the known and the lost, the acknowledged and the scattered — are reunited into one nation. Every descendant of the covenant people who was scattered by the Assyrian deportation (722 BCE), the Babylonian exile (586 BCE), the Roman destruction (70 CE), the Arab slave trade, the European slave trade — every bone in that valley is addressed by this prophecy. The dry bones are those whose identity was erased, whose history was stolen, whose connection to the covenant was declared dead by every institution. YHWH says: I will open your graves and raise you up.

faq.question: What does YHWH's denunciation of false shepherds (chapter 34) mean for institutional religion?
faq.answer: YHWH's indictment is specific and devastating: the shepherds eat the fat, clothe themselves with wool, slaughter the fattened sheep — but do not feed the flock (34:2-3). They do not strengthen the weak. They do not heal the sick. They do not bind up the injured. They do not bring back the strayed. They do not seek the lost. They rule with force and harshness. The flock is scattered because there is no shepherd (34:5). YHWH's response is not to reform the shepherds — it is to remove them: 'I am against the shepherds, and I will require my flock at their hand' (34:10). Then YHWH himself becomes the shepherd: 'I myself will search for my sheep and seek them out' (34:11). The institutional religious leadership that profits from the flock while the flock is scattered, sick, and lost is under YHWH's direct judgment. The Great Eraser operates through institutional shepherds who feed themselves — building denominations, accumulating wealth, gatekeeping access to the text — while the covenant people remain scattered and identity-stripped. YHWH does not ask the false shepherds to improve. He removes them.

faq.question: What is the Gog and Magog invasion and how does Qumran interpret it?
faq.answer: Chapters 38-39 describe a figure called Gog from the land of Magog — chief prince of Meshekh and Tuval — who leads a coalition of nations (Paras, Cush, Put, Gomer, Bet Togarmah) against Yisra'EL in the latter days. YHWH destroys the invasion with earthquake, pestilence, blood, torrential rain, hailstones, fire, and brimstone (38:19-22). The War Scroll at Qumran (1QM) draws on this framework — describing a forty-year eschatological war between the Sons of Light and the Sons of Darkness, with YHWH's direct intervention securing the final victory. The Qumran community understood the Gog prophecy not as past history but as future reality — a final confrontation between the covenant community and the nations aligned against them. Revelation 20:8 reuses the Gog and Magog names for the same eschatological scenario. The prophetic pattern is consistent: a final coalition moves against the covenant people, and YHWH — not human military power — destroys them.
